title: The Macro Minute: Should investors take some chips off the table because of tariffs?

our macro minute for Tuesday February
11th 2025 hope everyone's having a great

executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note as always if you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions as well as what specific
changes you need to make in your
portfolio you need to be a client of 42
macro so today's ke macro question is
should investors take some chips off the
table because of tariffs our answers are
no we do not believe investors should
ever adjust their positions because of
fundamental research views that is not
because fundamental research views are
not valuable they are rather it is
because fundamental research views are
almost always less effective at timing
inflections in momentum than
quantitative risk management signals we
built our kiss in Dr Mo signals for
investors who need help systematically
and dispassionately listening to and
responding to the market the results of
investors who follow our kiss and Dr Mo
signals are spectacular and significant
ific L better than most investors who do
not regarding the tariffs the EU has
already promised retaliatory measures in
response to the plan reciprocal tariffs
depreciating the euro is the best
strategy to offset the tariffs with
minimal disruption and reduce the risk
of a trade War we were among the first
on global Wall Street to call for Euro
US dollar parody last year and we
reiterate that view today additionally
Catherine man of the bank of England's
monetary policy Committee just confirmed
that she voted for a 50 basis point rate
cut at the most recent Bank of England
meeting recall that she was formerly the
most hawkish member of the MPC
descenting from the prior to rate
reductions of the current policy
normalization cycle all told a much
stronger US dollar seems inevitable amid
American exceptionalism and fresh
tariffs precisely when the US dollar
becomes strong enough to cause
significant problems for Global
liquidity and asset markets is for
kissing Dr moded aside not me we are
humble enough to know that we are not
smart enough to figure that out using
fundamental research alone are you
transition to our 42 micro dashboard as
always a wrap up with the question from
our community this one's uh on a couple
topics uh says morning everyone DD
thanks as always for the A+ weekend
content you're welcome thank you
appreciate you for partnering with us a
couple scenarios at are top of mind I
enjoy hearing your thoughts around these
General topics number one monetization
of us debt specifically comparing the US
possibilities to the Japan experience
and then number two uh a big if but if
bessent and president Trump are aligned
that the ultimate goal for now is a
reduction in the US 10year Treasury of
but president Trump wants to see the
stock market Not Crash how would one
attempt to thread that needle so number
one monetization of us debt uh on record
many years ago continue to be on record
that the US is not Japan uh we are a
twin deficit Nation uh that uh
foreigners own I want to say about 15 to
20% of our uh treasury bonds uh
marketable treasury market uh and more
more importantly we are a current
account deficit nation which means every
year we constantly need to attract
Capital to plug our net uh you know net
savings shortfall as an economy so uh
when you start moneti mon monetization
of US debt uh particularly the type of
monetization that we saw in Japan which
was in my opinion very deliberate and a
real kind of uh it wasn't really hidden
very well in terms of their true
intentions which was to reduce the
overall stock of of of of Japanese
government bonds on the marketplace you
know I don't think we can get away with
that here in the US uh uh for one we
would probably see a significant decline
uh in the US dollar recall that Japan
was in a balance sheet recession when
they were doing all this and so balance
sheet recession plus having you know
most of the B Government Bond Market be
owned by uh foreign investors
particularly foreign Banks which are
regulated by the bank of Japan in our
opinion we just don't have those same
types of Dynamics I want to say us
commercial Banks own about 15% of a US
market stry Market currently so they're
roughly just as big as foreign investors
but the reality is we need foreign
capital in a way that Japan which is the
current account uh Surplus Nation uh
does not so uh that in my opinion is the
biggest um challenge there because
ultimately would wind up with much lower
U if we went down the route of explicit
monetization it would wound up with a
much lower US dollar much higher levels
of inflation and ultimately probably be
counterproductive from the perspective
of having much higher uh interest rates
across the curve uh number two big
Essence so this U the question sort of
asks you know is there a sort of
um kind of a pushpull if you will uh
from the perspective of the president uh
and treasury secretary Scott besset
wanting lower 10year yields but the
president and I would not wanting a a
stock market crash in my opinion I don't
think those things are um mutually
exclusive I think us 10year yields down
from here especially in the context of
our uh inflation being uh too hot is
probably a better path forward for uh
the stock market at least from this
particular starting point uh given that
you know valuations uh concerns are are
pretty prevalent uh you know if you look
at our positioning model uh the
indicators within the positioning model
that are more sticky and stable over
time and thus more correlated with uh
the longer term kind of performance of
of of of asset markets a lot of those
longer term sticky stable indicators are
indicating that we are very much with in
a very full uh point in the positioning
cycle and so uh it yields much higher
from here will probably cause
significant problems yields lower from
here will probably take some pressure
off the system assuming yields aren't
you know declining because there's a a
high developing probability of a
recession uh which you know our models
continue to suggest that there's a very
low probability of a developing
recession in the US economy so again I
don't really think there's really much
needle threading um here in this
particular juncture so we'll wrap it up
